tossups.question: At a party hosted by the Selznicks, this author asked John Wayne, “what do you do for a living?” This author once reenacted the opening of The Killers over a drink with his biographer Alfred Appel Jr., whose son Richard is the showrunner of Family Guy. In an adaptation of a novel by this author, a drunk man wearing a bedsheet like a toga is shot after playing “Roman table tennis.” Questions like “do they teach you how to feel, finger to finger?” precede phrases written by this author like “interlinked” and “a tall white fountain played” in the (*) replicant baseline tests in Blade Runner 2049. This author wrote a 400-page screenplay for a 1962 film version of his best-known novel, which a director who shared his passion for chess heavily edited to conform to the Hays Code. The poster of that film depicts heart-shaped sunglasses under the text “how did they ever make a film of [his best-known novel]?”. For 10 points, Stanley Kubrick adapted what author’s novel Lolita?
tossups.question_sanitized: At a party hosted by the Selznicks, this author asked John Wayne, "what do you do for a living?" This author once reenacted the opening of The Killers over a drink with his biographer Alfred Appel Jr., whose son Richard is the showrunner of Family Guy. In an adaptation of a novel by this author, a drunk man wearing a bedsheet like a toga is shot after playing "Roman table tennis." Questions like "do they teach you how to feel, finger to finger?" precede phrases written by this author like "interlinked" and "a tall white fountain played" in the (*) replicant baseline tests in Blade Runner 2049. This author wrote a 400-page screenplay for a 1962 film version of his best-known novel, which a director who shared his passion for chess heavily edited to conform to the Hays Code. The poster of that film depicts heart-shaped sunglasses under the text "how did they ever make a film of [his best-known novel]?". For 10 points, Stanley Kubrick adapted what author's novel Lolita?
tossups.answer_sanitized: Vladimir Nabokov [or Vladimir Vladimirovich Nabokov; or Vladimir Sirin]

tossups.question: A story by this author consists of a letter to a “lady novelist” whom the narrator accuses of fictionalizing his own first love, but with all the facts wrong. This proponent of the “divine detail” wrote a story whose narrator fails to confess his love to the wife of a mediocre expatriate writer before a car crash kills her at the title “cloudy and dull” seaside town. A book of this author’s “dozen” collects a story that ends with a man reading the labels of jelly jars as a phone rings, as well as (*) “Spring in Fialta.” This author implied there were two plots in a story about a couple whose son is treated for “referential mania.” This author switched to English for the story “Signs and Symbols” and a novel in which he anagrammed himself as Vivian Darkbloom. For 10 points, what Russian émigré created Humbert Humbert?
tossups.question_sanitized: A story by this author consists of a letter to a "lady novelist" whom the narrator accuses of fictionalizing his own first love, but with all the facts wrong. This proponent of the "divine detail" wrote a story whose narrator fails to confess his love to the wife of a mediocre expatriate writer before a car crash kills her at the title "cloudy and dull" seaside town. A book of this author's "dozen" collects a story that ends with a man reading the labels of jelly jars as a phone rings, as well as (*) "Spring in Fialta." This author implied there were two plots in a story about a couple whose son is treated for "referential mania." This author switched to English for the story "Signs and Symbols" and a novel in which he anagrammed himself as Vivian Darkbloom. For 10 points, what Russian emigre created Humbert Humbert?
tossups.answer: Vladimir Nabokov [or Vladimir Vladimirovich Nabokov; accept Vladimir Sirin; accept Nabokov’s Dozen] (The first sentence refers to “The Admiralty Spire.”)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Vladimir Nabokov [or Vladimir Vladimirovich Nabokov; accept Vladimir Sirin; accept Nabokov's Dozen] (The first sentence refers to "The Admiralty Spire.")

tossups.question: In a story by this author, which is narrated to Miss K.I.T., Burmin falls to his knees in front of Maria after realizing she is the woman he jokingly married four years prior during the title event. While translating a work by this author, an author whose essays are collected in Strong Opinions wrote a set of “Notes on Prosody.” This author of “The Blizzard” wrote a collection framed as the stories of the mysterious landowner Ivan Belkin. Vladimir Nabokov feuded with Edmund Wilson over a translation of a work by this author composed of sonnets ending with masculine and feminine rhymes. This author wrote a poem in which Lensky challenges the title dandy to a duel after Tatyana’s name-day celebration. For 10 points, name this Russian author of Eugene Onegin.
tossups.question_sanitized: In a story by this author, which is narrated to Miss K.I.T., Burmin falls to his knees in front of Maria after realizing she is the woman he jokingly married four years prior during the title event. While translating a work by this author, an author whose essays are collected in Strong Opinions wrote a set of "Notes on Prosody." This author of "The Blizzard" wrote a collection framed as the stories of the mysterious landowner Ivan Belkin. Vladimir Nabokov feuded with Edmund Wilson over a translation of a work by this author composed of sonnets ending with masculine and feminine rhymes. This author wrote a poem in which Lensky challenges the title dandy to a duel after Tatyana's name-day celebration. For 10 points, name this Russian author of Eugene Onegin.
tossups.answer: Alexander Pushkin [or Alexander Sergeyevich Pushkin] (Vladimir Nabokov’s essays are collected in Strong Opinions.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Alexander Pushkin [or Alexander Sergeyevich Pushkin] (Vladimir Nabokov's essays are collected in Strong Opinions.)

tossups.question: In a story by this author, a female Devil offers a man a chance to select a harem of eager women for the afterlife, but only if he chooses an odd number of them. In another story by this author, a biology professor scares his much younger wife to death by placing a skeleton in their bed. In a third story by this author, a man gets trapped inside a portrait by Sebastiano del Piombo. The title fearsome creature awakens from an ancient slumber only to be used as an advertising marquee for competing tobacco companies in this author’s story “The Dragon.” Some of this author’s earliest stories, which often focused on fantasy creatures in the contemporary world, were published under the name (*) V. Sirin. Among this author’s most anthologized stories is one in which a boy suffers from “referential mania” as he thinks everything in the world somehow relates back to him, and another in which an acrostic and spiritualist influence explains a reference to icicles and parking meters. For 10 points, name this author of the short stories “Signs and Symbols” and “The Vane Sisters.”

Some of this author's earliest stories, which often focused on fantasy creatures in the contemporary world, were published under the name (*) V. Sirin. Among this author's most anthologized stories is one in which a boy suffers from "referential mania" as he thinks everything in the world somehow relates back to him, and another in which an acrostic and spiritualist influence explains a reference to icicles and parking meters. For 10 points, name this author of the short stories "Signs and Symbols" and "The Vane Sisters."

tossups.question: One work by this critic describes Ezra Pound as a “total fake” and a “venerable fraud” as well as calling Hemingway and Conrad “writers of books for boys.” In a letter, this critic declared he disliked Jane Austen and was “prejudiced, in fact, against all women writers.” Many of the works of this author of Strong Opinions were edited by his wife Vera. This Cornell professor created a quiz to determine whether his students were “good” or “bad” (*) readers. This critic praised The Double despite “intensely [disliking]” other works by Dostoyevsky. This critic’s unrhymed, literal translation of Eugene Onegin sparked a feud with his once friend Edmund Wilson. For 10 points, name this critic, who wrote Pale Fire and Lolita.
tossups.question_sanitized: One work by this critic describes Ezra Pound as a "total fake" and a "venerable fraud" as well as calling Hemingway and Conrad "writers of books for boys." This critic's unrhymed, literal translation of Eugene Onegin sparked a feud with his once friend Edmund Wilson. For 10 points, name this critic, who wrote Pale Fire and Lolita.

tossups.question: This author termed the swerving way Austen depicts Fanny Price's emotions the "knight's move" in a reading of Mansfield Park that stresses its "play theme." This author, whose favorite word is "mauve" according to Ben Blatt, suggested that instructors prepare diagrams of Leopold Bloom's Dublin in his Lectures on Literature. "The cradle rocks above an abyss" in this author's memoir, which discusses the governess Mademoiselle O. In his translation of a 1833 poem that includes the essay "Notes on Prosody," this author removed masculine and feminine rhymes from each of the poem's 14-line stanzas. This translator of Eugene Onegin wrote a novel in which a poem about how "I was the shadow of the waxwing slain" is erroneously interpreted to be about Zembla by Charles Kinbote. For 10 points, name this author of Speak, Memory; Pale Fire; and Lolita.

For 10 points, name this author of Speak, Memory; Pale Fire; and Lolita.

tossups.question: When asked what scenes he would liked to have filmed, this critic replied “Herman Melville at breakfast, feeding a sardine to his cat.” This critic urged readers to absorb a book’s “stereographic charm” by creating detailed maps of Mansfield Park and Dr. Jekyll’s London. To introduce a series on the great European novels, this critic dismissed “bad readers” who use fiction “to learn something about life in gay Paree or in sad Russia.” A novel by this critic opens with the parody quotation (+) “all happy families are more or less dissimilar; all unhappy ones are more or less alike.” This critic consistently rated Tolstoy as great, Turgenev as “pleasant,” and Dostoevsky as “mediocre.” This critic feuded with his erstwhile friend Edmund Wilson, who criticized his overly literal prose translation of (*) Eugene Onegin. For 10 points, name this author whose 19th century literary homages include Ada and a novel about the poet John Shade.

tossups.question: This author experienced an “absolutely Homeric retching" during a hospital stay which he escaped by jumping out the fire escape in a hospital gown. This author included maps of Leopold Bloom's Dublin and sketches of the beetle Gregor Samsa in his collected "Lectures on Literature." He created a character who loses his luggage with his papers, which may be the wrong ones, while giving a guest lecture, and is insulted by the book's narrator V. V. This author of (*) Pnin (“pa-NEEN”) worked his interest in chess into his character Luzhin in The Defense. This lepidopterist described his synesthesia in his autobiography Speak, Memory, and created a professor who wrote a 999-line poem beginning "I was the shadow of the waxwing slain." That poem by John Shade is annotated by the allegedly Zemblan professor Charles Kinbote. For 10 points, name this author of Pale Fire.

tossups.question: A poem in one of this author’s novels calls the title character “Wanted, wanted” and ends by stating “And the rest is rust and stardust.” This author wrote about a man who envisions a “white fountain” while reading a poem titled for a line from Timon of Athens. One of this author’s protagonists hands over a death sentence resembling T. S. Eliot’s “Ash Wednesday” before shooting his nemesis; the childhood love of that protagonist dies of typhus and is named for Edgar Allan Poe’s poem (*) “Annabel Lee.” In a novel by this author, the line “I was the shadow of the waxwing slain” opens a 999-line poem by John Shade. The playwright Clare Quilty pursues this author’s best-known character, the 12-year-old Dolores Haze. For 10 points, what author wrote about the pedophile Humbert Humbert’s love for a “nymphet” in his novel Lolita?

The playwright Clare Quilty pursues this author's best-known character, the 12-year-old Dolores Haze. For 10 points, what author wrote about the pedophile Humbert Humbert's love for a "nymphet" in his novel Lolita?

tossups.question: A different author’s poem “Racing Stars” describes [emphasize] this author composing a poem in which the speaker’s chest is ripped open by a snake, who deposits a “blazing shard of coal” before being called to “burn Man’s heart” with God’s word. This author of “The Prophet” died “with lead in breast and vengeful fire” due to “gossip” according to a poem that opens “The Bard is dead!” That poem about this author is titled (*) “The Death of the Poet.” A narrative poem by this author contrasts the “eternal sleep” of the nature-conquering Peter with a river that “hurls itself” on the city that Peter built. In that poem, a man witnesses the demise of his beloved Parasha in the flood of the Neva before being chased by the title statue. For 10 points, name this author of “The Bronze Horseman.”
tossups.answer: Alexander Pushkin (“Racing Stars” is by Vladimir Nabokov.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Alexander Pushkin ("Racing Stars" is by Vladimir Nabokov.)

tossups.question: The “beings of uncertain provenance and purpose” that appear in this author’s works are the subject of the essay “Dream Textures,” which appears in another author’s collection Campo Santo. After a fictionalized version of this author meets the painter Max Ferber on a mountain in Montreux, Ferber obsesses over creating a portrait of him. This author wrote some “notes on prosody” as an appendix to a work that inspired a feud with Edmund Wilson, this man’s translation of Eugene Onegin. This writer described his encounter with an old, fat swan to presage the death of his Swiss-French governess, Mademoiselle O, in his autobiography Speak, Memory. In this man’s best-known novel, the playwright Clare Quilty is murdered by the narrator, who abducts his step-daughter Dolores Haze. For 10 points, name this Russian-American author who invented Humbert Humbert in his novel Lolita.

In this man's best-known novel, the playwright Clare Quilty is murdered by the narrator, who abducts his step-daughter Dolores Haze. For 10 points, name this Russian-American author who invented Humbert Humbert in his novel Lolita.

tossups.question: A story by this author follows a couple whose institutionalized son thinks that “everything is a cipher and of everything he is the theme” due to his “referential mania.” In one of his stories, the narrator catches sight of a shadowless icicle and a red-shadowed parking meter; the last paragraph reveals that those sights were caused by the ghosts of Cynthia and Sybil. This author of “Signs and Symbols” describes seeing an old swan trying to climb into a boat at the end of a piece about his Swiss governess. This author of “The (*) Vane Sisters” included “Mademoiselle O” in a memoir that also discusses his love of butterfly-collecting. In a novel by this author, the protagonist shoots the playwright of The Enchanted Hunters, Clare Quilty, for kidnapping the title character. For 10 points, name this author of Speak, Memory and Lolita.

For 10 points, name this author of Speak, Memory and Lolita.

tossups.question: The title character of one novel by this author becomes confused between football and soccer and wonders about the height of his ex-wife’s son, Victor. That character, an émigré who is baffled by “unpredictable America,” is a professor at Waindell College. Another of his works sees an ugly daughter of Sybil drown herself after an unsuccessful date with Pete Dean. He wrote about (*) butterflies and synesthesia in an “Autobiogrpahy revisited” titled Speak, Memory. This author of Pnin wrote a novel that consists of commentaries by Charles Kinbote and a 999-line poem by John Shade. For 10 points, name this author of Pale Fire.

parts: Name this author of the book Lectures on Literature whose proctoring and office hours were handled by his wife Véra. While teaching at Wellesley, this author published his novel The Real Life of Sebastian Knight.

Nabokov spent most of his academic career at this university, where he taught Thomas Pynchon. Another of his students, Richard Fariña, fictionalized this university in his novel Been Down So Long It Looks Like Up to Me.

On a lecture tour, Nabokov quizzed students on traits held by people good at this action. Nabokov advocated for the “close” (“clohss”) form of this action, which one is often advised to do “like a writer.”

parts: Name this Russian-born author of Lolita.

Before writing Pnin, Nabokov wrote this dystopian novel set in a land ruled by the ‘Party of the Average Man’. This novel features many references to the ‘left’, such as the left side of the moon appearing in shade, and a guard shooting off part of a philosopher’s left ear.

Barth questioned the usefulness of novels within this broad literary genre. The scholar James Wood added the word “hysterical” to the name of this genre to categorize novels such as David Foster Wallace’s Infinite Jest and Zadie Smith’s White Teeth.

bonuses.leadin: The narrator asserts that, knowing the author’s “subtle sense of harmonic balance,” this line “would have completed the symmetry of the structure” of the poem it belongs to. For 10 points each:
parts: [emphasize] Describe this line of poetry that was left unwritten because its author was shot by the supposedly insane assassin Jack Grey, who was actually aiming for a former king.

The fictional Charles Kinbote argued that the unwritten thousandth line mirrored the first line about “the waxwing slain” in John Shade’s poem Pale Fire, which titles a novel by this author of Lolita.

This poet’s image of an owl banking to pass the window glass inspired the “waxwing slain” by the windowpane. Michael Wood compared Shade’s “richly rhymed life” to a spider in this writer’s poem “Design.”
answers: the last line of Pale Fire [or line 1,000 of Pale Fire; prompt on the missing line of Pale Fire or unfinished line of Pale Fire; prompt on “I was the shadow of the waxwing slain” by asking “which line of the poem?”]
Robert Frost [or Robert Lee Frost] (The first poem is “Questioning Faces.”)
answers_sanitized: the last line of Pale Fire [or line 1,000 of Pale Fire; prompt on the missing line of Pale Fire or unfinished line of Pale Fire; prompt on "I was the shadow of the waxwing slain" by asking "which line of the poem?"]

Robert Frost [or Robert Lee Frost] (The first poem is "Questioning Faces.")

parts: Name this play by the author of The Entertainer. This 1956 play focuses on the working-class Jimmy Porter and his middle-class wife Alison, who play “bears and squirrels” upon reuniting.
Nora is belittlingly called a “little squirrel” by her husband Torvald, whom she later leaves by symbolically slamming the door in this Henrik Ibsen play.

Squirrels frequently appear as portents of misfortune for the title character of this novel. This novel’s protagonist recalls the death at Buchenwald of his former lover Mira Belochkin, whose last name means “squirrel.”
answers: A Doll’s House [or A Doll House; or Et dukkehjem]

"The Vane Sisters" has one of these narrators. Exemplified by Humbert Humbert in Lolita, these narrators provide 1st-person accounts which cannot be trusted due to character flaw.

Schiff’s biography recounts how, on several occasions, Véra Nabokov stopped her husband from burning this novel, which is about Humbert Humbert’s obsession with the title “nymphet.”
The last chapter of this memoir by Vladimir Nabokov is presented as a second-person address to Véra. This memoir includes chapters on Nabokov’s mother and on his passion for butterflies.

parts: Name this novel written in a form of iambic tetrameter that alternates masculine and feminine rhymes, known as its title character’s “stanza.” Its title character incites a duel when he attends Tatyana’s name day celebration.
This writer earned Wilson’s ire for his extremely literal translation of Eugene Onegin, which he accompanied with an analysis of Russian and English verse called Notes on Prosody (“PRAH-zuh-dee”).

In Notes on Prosody, Nabokov writes that the beauty of this device in English is “the delicate sensation of something being physically preserved by the voice” and finds that it is absent in Russian. Robert Bridges explains syllabic variation between lines in Paradise Lost with this device in Milton’s Prosody.

bonuses.leadin: For 10 points each, answer some questions about a famously difficult literary task.
parts: The first people to perform this task were the husband-and-wife team Edwin and Willa Muir, working in the 1930s. Princeton professor Stanley Corngold has devoted most of his career to carrying out this task.

One of the key tasks for a Kafka translator is determining how to interpret this German word in the first sentence of The Metamorphosis. Literally meaning “an animal unfit for sacrifice”, this word has been translated as “vermin”, “insect”, “cockroach”, and even “bedbug”.

This author argued that translations such as “cockroach” were objectively incorrect due to the physical description offered in the text, concluding “He is merely a big beetle.” His own translation work included getting into a fight with Edmund Wilson over the proper way to translate Eugene Onegin from his native Russian.

His own translation work included getting into a fight with Edmund Wilson over the proper way to translate Eugene Onegin from his native Russian.

answers_sanitized: the duel between Eugene Onegin and Vladimir Lensky [or the duel in the poem Eugene Onegin; or obvious equivalents]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: This event is planned after one of its participants flirts with Olga at the name day ceremony of Olga's sister Tatiana. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this fatal incident related in fourteen-line stanzas, which Guillot and Zaretsky, the seconds, do not prevent. It is survived by a dandyish St. Petersburg socialite.
This part-black poet of The Bronze Horseman wrote the duel scene as part of his long poem Eugene Onegin.
This author of King, Queen, Knave harshly criticized Walter Arndt's English translation of Eugene Onegin before producing his own.

bonuses.leadin: In one work by this author, Nikolai cheats with Sasha Lebedev and shoots himself right as the rest of the cast jumps to his defense against the doctor Eugene Lvov. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this author of Ivanov who also wrote about the affair between Dmitri and Anna in his short story “The Lady with the Dog”.

In this play by Chekhov, Lyuba Ranevsky returns home to Russia after living in France for five years. This play concludes with Lyuba selling her family’s estate to Lopakhin after refusing to build cottages on it.

This other author both praised and criticized Chekhov’s prose writings in his Lectures on Russian Literature. The planet of Anti-Terra provides the setting to a novel by him in which Van Veen falls in love with his sister.
